title: Preparing walls for tiling
subtitle: Check and correct adhesion, flatness, and stability before any tile goes up
excerpt: The foundation work before wall tiling. Covers assessing the existing surface, removing loose material, dealing with painted surfaces and plasterboard, and applying a stabiliser or primer where needed.
sourceNotes: BS 5385-1:2009 Wall and floor tiling. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A check made by pressing a thumb firmly onto the existing surface and then sliding it. A surface that drags the thumb (high adhesion) is usually tileable; a surface that feels polished or glazed needs mechanical keying or a bonding agent.
A coat of bonding agent or stabilising primer applied to a slick or dusty surface before tiling. Increases adhesion so the tile adhesive bonds properly.

content.text: A failed tile is almost always a preparation failure, not an adhesive failure. The adhesive is only as good as the surface it sticks to, and a tile that drops at 2 a.m. from a bathroom wall that was not properly keyed is the outcome of skipping this stage. Work through these checks in order before any adhesive is spread.
When to use this technique

Every tiling job. Even in a new kitchen with fresh plaster, the preparation checks take 30 minutes and prevent a day's work undone. The process applies to ceramic, porcelain, stone, and glass tiles, and to adhesive mortars and organic mastic adhesives.
Step 1, Check the surface for soundness

Tap the wall firmly with a knuckle and listen for a hollow sound. Hollow plaster is delaminated from the backing and will pull away when the tile adhesive load is applied. Remove all hollow sections back to solid ground. Fill small areas with bonding plaster or one-coat filler; for large voids, cut back to bare brick or blockwork and replaster.
Step 2, Check flatness

Hold the spirit level across the wall horizontally and vertically at several positions. Variations greater than 3 mm over any 1800 mm run will cause grout joints to visibly widen or narrow and may prevent full tile contact. Fill hollows with filler or bonding coat; knock down ridges with a scraper. Major undulations need skim plaster.
Step 3, Assess adhesion
Run the
adhesion test

across the full tiling area. Painted surfaces need mechanical preparation: sand with coarse paper (60 to 80 grit) to remove the gloss, particularly on gloss or satin-finish paint. Fully tiled areas being re-tiled need the old tiles removed and the surface assessed underneath. Leaving old tiles as a base is acceptable only where they are fully bonded and the wall can carry the extra weight, check by doing the knock test across all old tiles.
Step 4, Prime or key the surface where needed

Fresh plaster must dry for at least 4 weeks before tiling. If that is not possible, use a tile-on-fresh-plaster adhesive or apply a PVA bonding agent at 1:1 dilution and let it become tacky before spreading adhesive. Plasterboard: fix joints with scrim tape and jointing compound, leave to cure, then apply a full coat of
key coat

(PVA or specialist tile primer) across the entire surface. Dusty or sandy surfaces: stabilise with diluted PVA before tiling, any surface that rubs off on a wiped hand is too friable for tile adhesive.
What it looks like when it's right

A properly prepared wall is firm, flat to within 3 mm, sound throughout (no hollow areas), and slightly textured to the touch. The primer or bonding agent has dried to a matte skin that does not rub off. Running the filling knife across the surface produces no chattering on ridges or soft patches.
Common mistakes
Tiling directly over painted gloss without sanding: the adhesive grips the paint, not the plaster. The whole tile-and-paint layer peels as a sheet when stressed.
Skipping the flatness check: this shows up most around window reveals and bathroom alcoves where the plasterwork was rushed. Tile adhesive cannot hide a large undulation.
Tiling onto fresh plaster before it has fully dried: residual moisture pushes the adhesive off the wall from behind. The tiles drop weeks or months after installation.
